Question: The speed limit on some interstate highways is roughly 100 km/h. (a) What is this in meters per second? (b) How many miles per hour is this?
o3 Answer:
The posted speed limit is 100 km/h.

(a) Convert to meters per second  

100 km/h × (1000 m / 1 km) × (1 h / 3600 s)  
= 100 000 m / 3600 s  
≈ 27.8 m/s  

(With two significant figures from “100 km/h”, this is 28 m/s.)

(b) Convert to miles per hour  

Use 1 mi = 1.609 km (or equivalently 1 km = 0.621 mi):

100 km/h × (0.621 mi / 1 km)  
≈ 62.1 mi/h  

(Again, to two significant figures: 62 mi/h.)

Known conversion factors  
1 km = 1000 m ⟹ 1 = 1 km / 1000 m  
1 h   = 3600 s ⟹ 1 = 3600 s / 1 h

Start with 1.0 m/s:

1.0 m/s × (1 km / 1000 m) × (3600 s / 1 h)

Now cancel the units:

• metres cancel:  m in the numerator with m in the denominator  
• seconds cancel: s in the numerator with s in the denominator

The numerical part becomes

1.0 × 3600 / 1000 = 3.6

What is left is the desired unit km/h, so

1.0 m/s = 3.6 km/h.

Therefore, a speed of one metre per second is exactly equal to 3.6 kilometres per hour.

Question: Tectonic plates are large segments of the Earth’s crust that move slowly. Suppose that one such plate has an average speed of 4.0 cm/year. (a) What distance does it move in 1 s at this speed? (b) What is its speed in kilometers per million years?
o3 Answer:
Given speed of the plate  
v = 4.0 cm / year

(a) Distance traveled in 1 s  

Convert the speed to centimetres per second:

1 yr = 365 d × 24 h × 3600 s ≈ 3.156 × 10⁷ s

v = 4.0 cm/yr = 4.0 cm ÷ 3.156 × 10⁷ s  
  ≈ 1.27 × 10⁻⁷ cm s⁻¹

Distance in 1 s:  
d = v Δt = (1.27 × 10⁻⁷ cm s⁻¹)(1 s) = 1.27 × 10⁻⁷ cm

Expressed in other units:  
1.27 × 10⁻⁷ cm = 1.27 × 10⁻⁹ m ≈ 1.3 nm

So the plate moves only about 1 nanometre in one second.

(b) Speed in kilometres per million years

1 Myr (million years) = 1.0 × 10⁶ yr  
1 km = 10⁵ cm

v = 4.0 cm/yr × (1 km / 10⁵ cm) × (10⁶ yr / 1 Myr)

   = 4.0 × 10⁶ / 10⁵ km / Myr  
   = 4.0 × 10¹ km / Myr  
   = 40 km / Myr

Hence

(a) ≈ 1.3 × 10⁻⁷ cm ≈ 1.3 nm in 1 s  
(b) 40 km per million years

Question: Approximately how many atoms thick is a cell membrane, assuming all atoms there average about twice the size of a hydrogen atom?
